stage.name: Trading Fundamentals
stage.description: Order books, RSI/MACD/Bollinger, Kelly Criterion, perpetuals, Sharpe ratio, and trading psychology.

elements.content: How prices are discovered on centralized exchanges

Order Types

Market order: fills immediately at best available price — guarantees execution, not price

Limit order: specify max buy / min sell price — sits in book, may not fill if price moves away

Stop-limit: triggers limit order when stop price hit — useful for exits, can slip through in fast markets

Iceberg order: large order with visible small quantity, hidden remainder — institutional tool

Market Microstructure

Bid-ask spread = maker profit; taker fee + spread = total cost of round trip

Market makers profit by buying bid, selling ask — need high fill rate to profit on small spread

Price discovery: continuous auction — best bid and ask meet to create last trade price

Level 2 data: full order book depth shows where liquidity exists and where price may bounce or break

Exchange Mechanics

Matching engine: FIFO at each price level — first order at price gets filled first

Maker-taker fee model: makers (limit orders) get rebate, takers (market orders) pay fee

Hidden liquidity: dark pools and internal crossing let institutions trade without moving markets

Wash trading: illegal self-dealing to inflate volume — common on unregulated exchanges, watch for it
RSI, MACD, Bollinger Bands — math and interpretation

RSI

RSI = 100 − 100/(1 + RS) where RS = avg gain / avg loss over 14 periods

Overbought: RSI > 70 — price may mean-revert but can stay overbought in strong trend

Oversold: RSI < 30 — potential buy zone but works better in ranging markets than trending

Divergence: price makes new high but RSI makes lower high = bearish divergence (weakening momentum)

MACD

MACD line = EMA(12) − EMA(26); Signal line = EMA(9) of MACD line

Histogram = MACD − Signal: above zero = bullish momentum, below = bearish

Crossover signal: MACD crosses above signal = buy; below = sell — lagging, use with other signals

Zero-line cross: MACD crosses above 0 = trend confirmation; below = trend reversal

Bollinger Bands

Middle band = 20-period SMA; Upper/Lower = SMA ± 2 standard deviations

Price touching upper band ≠ automatic sell — in strong uptrend, price 'walks the band'

Bollinger Squeeze: bands narrow = volatility compression = breakout incoming (direction unknown)

%B: measures price position within bands — 1.0 = upper band, 0 = lower, 0.5 = middle

Kelly Criterion

f* = (bp − q) / b where b = net odds, p = win probability, q = 1 − p

Maximizes long-run geometric growth rate — but full Kelly leads to massive drawdowns (−50% swings)

Half-Kelly or Quarter-Kelly is standard practice — reduces growth slightly but cuts variance dramatically

Example: 60% win rate, 1.5:1 reward/risk → full Kelly = 26.7% of capital per trade

Position Sizing

Fixed fractional: risk a fixed % of account per trade (e.g., 1-2%) — account size adjusts bet size automatically

ATR sizing: position size = (account × risk%) / (ATR × multiplier) — scales to current volatility

Correlation adjustment: two correlated positions = 1 effective position for risk management purposes

Maximum position: even with high conviction, cap individual positions at 10-20% of portfolio

Drawdown Management

Maximum drawdown (MDD): largest peak-to-trough decline — critical for evaluating strategies

100% return needed to recover from 50% drawdown; 25% needed to recover from 20%

Circuit breakers: stop trading after losing X% in a day/week — prevents revenge trading death spiral

Sharpe ratio: (return − risk-free rate) / std deviation — >1 is acceptable, >2 is excellent

Perp Mechanics

Perpetual = futures contract with no expiry — tracks spot via funding rate mechanism

Funding rate paid every 8 hours: if funding > 0, longs pay shorts; if < 0, shorts pay longs

Mark price = index price + EMA of basis — used for liquidations, not last traded price

Open interest: total notional value of outstanding contracts — rising OI + rising price = bullish

Leverage & Margin

Initial margin: deposit required to open position (e.g., 10% for 10× leverage)

Maintenance margin: minimum equity to keep position open (e.g., 5% for 10× leverage)

Liquidation: position closed when equity falls to maintenance margin level

Auto-deleveraging (ADL): if insurance fund insufficient, profitable traders' positions are reduced

Basis Trading

Basis = futures price − spot price; positive basis = contango (bullish)

Cash-and-carry: buy spot + short perp → collect funding rate — delta-neutral yield strategy

Funding arbitrage: when perp trades at premium, short perp + long spot = risk-free funding income

Negative funding (backwardation): shorts pay longs — historically precedes price bottoms

Sharpe Ratio

Sharpe = (Rp − Rf) / σp where Rp = portfolio return, Rf = risk-free rate, σp = std dev

Annualized: multiply daily Sharpe by √252 (trading days)

Interpretation: <1 poor, 1-2 acceptable, >2 excellent, >3 exceptional

Limitation: treats upside and downside volatility equally — high Sharpe can hide fat-tail risk

Alternative Metrics

Sortino ratio: uses only downside std deviation — better for strategies with positive skew

Calmar ratio: annualized return / max drawdown — good for comparing trend-following strategies

Maximum adverse excursion (MAE): worst loss during a winning trade — key for stop placement

Win rate × avg win / avg loss = expectancy — must be positive for profitable strategy

Backtesting Pitfalls

Overfitting: too many parameters → fits historical noise, fails on new data → use out-of-sample test

Lookahead bias: accidentally using future data to generate signals → inflated backtest results

Survivorship bias: testing only assets that still exist → excludes coins that went to zero

Transaction costs: include spread, slippage, and fees — can turn profitable backtest into loser

Key Biases

Loss aversion: losing $1000 hurts ~2× more than gaining $1000 feels good — causes holding losers too long

Confirmation bias: seek information confirming existing position — actively seek disconfirmation

Recency bias: overweight recent events — bull market makes everyone think prices only go up

Disposition effect: selling winners too early, holding losers too long — opposite of 'let winners run'

Emotional Discipline

Trading journal: record every trade with reasoning, emotion state, and outcome — patterns emerge

Rule-based trading: predefined entry/exit rules remove in-the-moment emotion from decisions

Risk per trade: knowing max loss before entering removes panic during drawdown

Detach from P&L during trade: watching tick-by-tick creates anxiety; check at planned review times

Process Over Outcome

Good process + bad outcome (bad luck) ≠ bad decision — evaluate process, not just result

Expected value thinking: focus on probability-weighted outcome, not individual trade result

Poker analogy: bad beats happen to good players — discipline is playing correctly when results are random

Meditation and sleep: emotional regulation critically depends on physiological state — non-negotiable for traders

Strategy Types

Trend following: buy assets in uptrend, short downtrend — works across all asset classes historically

Mean reversion: bet on price returning to mean — works in ranging markets, fails in trending ones

Statistical arbitrage: exploit price discrepancies between correlated assets using pairs trading

Market making: continuously quote bid/ask, profit from spread — requires sophisticated inventory management

Execution Infrastructure

Co-location: server physically near exchange matching engine — reduces round-trip latency to <0.1ms

Direct market access (DMA): bypass broker, send orders directly to exchange — essential for HFT

Smart order routing (SOR): automatically route to best-priced venue across multiple exchanges

TWAP/VWAP execution: break large orders into smaller pieces over time to reduce market impact

Backtesting & Live

Walk-forward optimization: repeatedly re-optimize on expanding window, test on held-out data

Paper trading: run strategy in real-time with simulated money before risking capital

Slippage modeling: assume 50-100% of bid-ask spread as execution cost for realistic backtest

Kill switches: automatic position close if daily loss exceeds threshold — essential safety mechanism
